text: The surgeon opens the lateral space to the ureter, exposing the pararectal fossa. The left ureter is visible and appears enlarged due to myoma compression. The uterine artery is skeletonized for safe clip placement, with the ureter positioned just below the grasper.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the right side, opening the peritoneum lateral to the internal iliac artery and vein. The avascular retroperitoneal space is opened, revealing the internal iliac artery. Fatty tissue surrounding the internal iliac artery is removed, and the pararectal fossa is dissected using traction and counter-traction. The uterine artery, identified as the first medial branch of the anterior iliac artery, is clipped at its origin. This devascularizes the uterus, allowing for the removal of a large ten-centimeter type 2-5 myoma. The serosa surrounding the myoma is opened, followed by the myometrial capsule.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the myometrial capsule, maintaining a bloodless operative field. A ten millimeter forceps is used to apply traction on the ten centimeter myoma, facilitating its extraction through a seven centimeter hystereotomy. The assistant helps by retracting the normal serosa, and hydro dissection is employed to separate the surrounding tissue of the myoma.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the uterus and serosa, using water to enhance visibility of the dissecting plane. The intracavitary part of the myoma is treated with minimal energy application to spare the surrounding endometrium. The assistant applies water to the residual endometrium to reduce heat from the ultrasonic scalpel. The endometrium is clearly visible, and traction is used to remove the intracavitary myoma while preserving the myometrial capsule.

The surgeon identifies and respects normal tissue while minimizing the use of harmonic energy. The uterus is completely removed from its bed, maintaining a bloodless field. The surgeon proceeds to close the myoma bed using stitches with a large 14mm needle, placing sutures two centimeters outside the uterine scar. The intracorporeal knotting technique is used after invaginating healthy myometrium into the myoma bed.

The surgeon locks the knot using intracorporeal knotting technique, ensuring the knot is placed two centimeters away from the myoma edges. A reverse knot is tied, taking a large bite of tissue to invaginate healthy tissue into the myoma bed. Assistants help invaginate the tissue, demonstrating effective anatomical reconstruction. V Quill sutures are used to tie the knot securely, with stitches placed every centimeter to restore normal anatomy and close the myoma bed.

The surgeon performs a double flat knot invagination technique, with the assistant locking the knot and the surgeon continuing the procedure. The final knot is placed more laterally to invaginate the residual healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, far from the myometrial scar. The assistants close the knot and invaginate the tissue. Morcellation is performed using a 15mm electromechanical morcellator in the left iliac fossa, set at 700 volts per minute, under direct and permanent vision. The final aspect of the surgery is shown.
